questions.question: What is the main purpose of TypeScript over JavaScript?

choices.a: It runs faster at runtime than JavaScript
choices.b: It adds optional static typing and compiles to JavaScript
choices.c: It replaces JavaScript in browsers natively
choices.d: It is a completely different language with no JS compatibility

questions.explanation:
TypeScript is a superset of JavaScript that adds optional static type annotations. It compiles (transpiles) to plain JavaScript, which means browsers and Node.js run the compiled JS output — TypeScript itself never executes directly.

The type system catches errors at compile time (before runtime), making large codebases more maintainable. TypeScript does NOT make code faster at runtime — the output is the same JS the engine runs.

questions.question: What is the difference between `type` and `interface` in TypeScript?

choices.a: `type` can only describe primitive types; `interface` can describe objects
choices.b: `interface` supports declaration merging; `type` can express unions, intersections, and mapped types
choices.c: They are completely identical with no differences
choices.d: `type` is deprecated and should not be used

questions.explanation:
Both `type` and `interface` can describe object shapes, but they have key differences:

• `interface` supports DECLARATION MERGING: if you declare the same interface twice, TypeScript merges them. This is essential for augmenting third-party types.
• `type` can express UNIONS (`type A = B | C`), INTERSECTIONS (`type A = B & C`), MAPPED TYPES (`type R = { [K in keyof T]: ... }`), CONDITIONAL TYPES, and TEMPLATE LITERAL TYPES — none of which are possible with `interface`.
• For simple object shapes, both work. Prefer `interface` for public APIs that consumers might extend; prefer `type` for complex computed shapes.

questions.question: What does `unknown` represent and how does it differ from `any`?

choices.a: `unknown` and `any` are identical — it's just a style preference
choices.b: `unknown` disables type checking entirely, `any` keeps it
choices.c: `unknown` is the type-safe counterpart of `any`: you must narrow it before using it

questions.explanation:
• `any` completely opts OUT of the type system. You can call methods, access properties, assign it anywhere — TypeScript simply stops checking. It is an escape hatch that removes safety.

• `unknown` is the TYPE-SAFE top type. Like `any`, it can hold any value. But unlike `any`, you CANNOT do anything with it until you narrow its type (e.g. with `typeof`, `instanceof`, or a type predicate). TypeScript forces you to verify what you have before using it.

Rule of thumb: never use `any` in new code; use `unknown` when you don't know the shape of external data, then narrow.

questions.question: What is the `never` type used for?

choices.a: It represents a value that will always be `null`
choices.b: It is the return type of functions that never return (throw or infinite loop) and marks unreachable code in exhaustiveness checks
choices.c: It is a synonym for `void` when a function returns undefined
choices.d: It is used only in conditional types to remove branches

questions.explanation:
`never` is the BOTTOM type — a subtype of every type but no type is a subtype of `never` (except `never` itself).

Two main uses:
1. FUNCTIONS THAT NEVER RETURN: `function fail(msg: string): never { throw new Error(msg); }` — the function always throws, so it never produces a value.
2. EXHAUSTIVENESS CHECKS: In a switch/if chain over a discriminated union, if all branches are handled, the `default` branch has type `never`. If you add a new union member without handling it, TypeScript reports an error because the variable is no longer `never`.

`void` means 'returns undefined (or nothing we care about)' — very different!

questions.question: What does `as const` do?

choices.a: It converts a value to a constant at runtime, preventing mutation
choices.b: It widens the type of a value to its base type
choices.c: It narrows literal types to their widest possible value
choices.d: It infers the narrowest possible literal types and marks the object as deeply readonly

questions.explanation:
`as const` is a CONST ASSERTION. It tells TypeScript to:
1. Infer the NARROWEST possible type: `let x = 'hello' as const` → type is `'hello'` not `string`.
2. Deeply mark all properties as `readonly`.

Without `as const`:
  `const colors = ['red', 'green']` → type is `string[]`

With `as const`:
  `const colors = ['red', 'green'] as const` → type is `readonly ['red', 'green']`

This is extremely useful for defining constants used as discriminants, config objects, and route definitions without losing type precision. Note: it's a TYPE-LEVEL annotation only — the JS output is unchanged.

questions.question: What is a type assertion (`value as SomeType`) and when is it dangerous?

choices.a: It converts a value from one type to another at runtime
choices.b: It is a compile-time hint to the type checker; it does NOT convert values at runtime and can cause runtime errors if wrong
choices.c: It is equivalent to a runtime cast like in Java or C#
choices.d: It can only be used to narrow, never to widen, a type

questions.explanation:
Type assertions (`as T`) are COMPILE-TIME ONLY. They tell TypeScript 'trust me, this value is of type T'. The emitted JavaScript has NO type-checking code — the value is used as-is.

Danger: if your assertion is wrong, you get runtime errors that TypeScript won't warn you about:
  `const x = someValue as string; x.toUpperCase(); // runtime crash if x is a number`

Rule: Only assert when you know more than TypeScript does (e.g. after an API call you've validated). Prefer TYPE GUARDS or proper narrowing instead.

The 'double assertion trick' (`value as unknown as OtherType`) is a red flag — it bypasses all safety.

questions.question: What is the difference between `void` and `undefined` as return types?

choices.b: `undefined` means the function explicitly returns `undefined`; `void` means the return value should not be used, but the function may still return `undefined`
choices.c: `void` means the function throws; `undefined` means it returns nothing

questions.explanation:
• `(): undefined` — TypeScript requires the function to explicitly return `undefined`. You must write `return undefined;` or just `return;`.

• `(): void` — TypeScript communicates that the CALLER should not use the return value. The function CAN return `undefined`, and can even technically return a value (which will be ignored). This is especially relevant for callbacks:
  `[1,2,3].forEach(x => x * 2)` — forEach expects `(x: number) => void`, which allows any function even if it returns a value.

void is used when you want to signal 'don't use the return value', not 'this function returns nothing'.

questions.question: What does `satisfies` do (TypeScript 4.9+)?

choices.a: It is an alias for `as` with runtime validation
choices.b: It checks that a value matches a type while preserving the value's narrowest inferred type
choices.c: It only works with interfaces, not type aliases
choices.d: It replaces the need for generic functions

questions.explanation:
`satisfies` (TS 4.9+) validates that a value CONFORMS to a type WITHOUT widening it to that type.

Problem with `as Type`:
  `const palette = { red: [255,0,0], green: '#00ff00' } as Record<string,string|number[]>`
  Now `palette.red` is `string | number[]` — you lost the tuple precision.

With `satisfies`:
  `const palette = { red: [255,0,0], green: '#00ff00' } satisfies Record<string,string|number[]>`
  `palette.red` is still `number[]` — TypeScript checked the constraint but kept the narrow type.

Best of both worlds: type safety of the constraint + precision of the inferred type.

questions.question: What is the `strictNullChecks` compiler option and why does it matter?

choices.a: It makes `null` and `undefined` assignable to any type (looser checking)
choices.b: It makes `null` and `undefined` distinct types — they cannot be assigned to other types without explicit handling
choices.d: It is enabled by default when you don't have a tsconfig.json

questions.explanation:
Without `strictNullChecks` (old default), `null` and `undefined` are subtypes of every type, so `const s: string = null` is valid. This is a massive source of bugs.

With `strictNullChecks: true` (or `strict: true`), `null` and `undefined` are their OWN distinct types:
  `const s: string = null; // Error!
  const s: string | null = null; // OK`

You must EXPLICITLY declare nullability and NARROW before using:
  `if (s !== null) { s.toUpperCase(); } // safe`

Always use `strict: true` in production projects. This single flag eliminates entire classes of null-pointer bugs.

questions.question: What does the `!` non-null assertion operator do?

choices.a: It throws an error at runtime if the value is null
choices.c: It tells TypeScript to treat the expression as non-null/non-undefined — no runtime check occurs

questions.explanation:
The postfix `!` is the NON-NULL ASSERTION OPERATOR. `value!` tells TypeScript 'I guarantee this is not null or undefined — remove those from the type'.

Important: it is COMPILE-TIME ONLY with NO runtime effect. If the value actually is null at runtime, you will get a runtime error.

Use it only when you are 100% sure the value cannot be null/undefined and TypeScript cannot prove it (e.g. after manual DOM checks, after third-party API calls).

Prefer optional chaining (`?.`) or explicit null checks for safety. The `!` operator is like saying 'I know better than TypeScript' — use sparingly.
